title: Psalms 72
subtitle: Of Solomon: The King’s Reign of Justice

sections.header: Verses 1–11 — “He Will Judge Your People in Righteousness”

heb.paragraph: "Endow the king with your justice (ṣedeq), O God, the royal son with your righteousness" (v.1). A prayer for Solomon that transcends Solomon. The ideal king judges the afflicted (v.2), defends the poor (v.4), and endures "as long as the sun" (v.5). "He will rule from sea to sea and from the River to the ends of the earth" (v.8). All kings will bow; all nations will serve (v.11). No historical king fulfilled this; it is messianic.

notes.note: "Endow the king with your justice. He will judge your people in righteousness, the afflicted with justice. He will defend the afflicted and save the children of the needy." The ideal king’s first concern: justice for the poor.
"He will endure as long as the sun. He will rule from sea to sea." Solar permanence + global dominion = messianic scope.
"All kings will bow down to him and all nations will serve him." The Psalm 2 vision fulfilled: nations submit to God’s anointed.

refs.note: Magi bringing gifts from the East fulfills the psalm's vision of Sheba and Seba presenting tribute.
The Branch judging the poor with righteousness develops the same royal justice theology.
The kingdom of the world becoming Christ's forever fulfills the psalm's eternal reign vision.

hist.context: Psalm 72 closes Book II of the Psalter with a royal psalm attributed to or for Solomon. The prayer for the king to 'defend the afflicted' and 'crush the oppressor' (v.4) defines ideal kingship. The geographical scope — from sea to sea, from the River to the ends of the earth (v.8) — exceeded any historical Israelite king, pointing toward messianic expectation.

sections.header: Verses 12–20 — “May the Whole Earth Be Filled with His Glory. Amen and Amen.”

heb.paragraph: "May the whole earth be filled with his glory (kābōd). Amen and Amen" (v.19). The king rescues the needy (v.12), takes pity on the weak (v.13), redeems from oppression (v.14). "Precious is their blood in his sight" (v.14). Grain will flourish, fruit will sway like Lebanon (v.16). His name will endure forever (v.17). "Through him all nations will be blessed" (v.17) — the Abrahamic promise (Gen 12:3). BOOK II DOXOLOGY: "Praise be to his glorious name forever; may the whole earth be filled with his glory. Amen and Amen. This concludes the prayers of David son of Jesse" (vv.19–20).

notes.note: "He will rescue the needy who cry out, the afflicted who have no one to help. He will take pity on the weak. Precious is their blood in his sight." The messianic king values every life. The poor are not expendable.
"Through him all nations will be blessed." The Abrahamic promise (Gen 12:3) applied to the king. Blessing flows from the king to the world.

"Praise be to the LORD God, the God of Israel, who alone does marvellous deeds. May the whole earth be filled with his glory. Amen and Amen." Book II’s closing doxology. The second of five. "This concludes the prayers of David son of Jesse."

refs.note: All nations blessed through Abraham's offspring fulfills the psalm's 'all nations will be blessed through him.'
Peter identifies Jesus as the offspring through whom all peoples are blessed.
Nations bringing their glory into the new Jerusalem completes the psalm's tribute vision.

hist.context: The closing doxology (vv.18-19) and note 'the prayers of David son of Jesse are ended' (v.20) mark the conclusion of an early Davidic collection. The psalm's description of abundance — grain on hilltops, fruit like Lebanon's cedars (v.16) — evokes Eden restored. Jewish tradition read this as messianic; Christian interpretation applied it to Christ's universal kingdom.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIVMay he endure as long as the sun, as long as the moon, through all generations.ESVMay they fear you while the sun endures, and as long as the moon, throughout all generations!NIVMay he defend the afflicted among the people and save the children of the needy; may he crush the oppressor.ESVMay he defend the cause of the poor of the people, give deliverance to the children of the needy, and crush the oppressor!

rows.label: Book II Closes — 72:12–20
rows.text: "Precious is their blood." "Through him all nations blessed." "AMEN AND AMEN." Glory fills the earth.

hebtext.note: "Endow the king with your justice (ṣedeq), O God, the royal son with your righteousness" (v.1). A prayer for Solomon that transcends Solomon. The ideal king judges the afflicted (v.2), defends the poor (v.4), and endures "as long as the sun" (v.5). "He will rule from sea to sea and from the River to the ends of the earth" (v.8). All kings will bow; all nations will serve (v.11). No historical king fulfilled this; it is messianic.

"May the whole earth be filled with his glory (kābōd). Amen and Amen" (v.19). The king rescues the needy (v.12), takes pity on the weak (v.13), redeems from oppression (v.14). "Precious is their blood in his sight" (v.14). Grain will flourish, fruit will sway like Lebanon (v.16). His name will endure forever (v.17). "Through him all nations will be blessed" (v.17) — the Abrahamic promise (Gen 12:3). BOOK II DOXOLOGY: "Praise be to his glorious name forever; may the whole earth be filled with his glory. Amen and Amen. This concludes the prayers of David son of Jesse" (vv.19–20).

themes.note: Prophecy peaks at 10: messianic king, Abrahamic blessing, global reign. Justice and Sovereignty at 9: the ideal king judges the poor. Covenant at 9: Abrahamic promise applied to the king. Mission at 8: all nations blessed. Book II’s climax.
